title: Applying micro-cement to a bathroom floor
subtitle: Apply a thin-coat cementitious finish over an existing tiled or concrete floor to achieve a seamless, contemporary look
excerpt: How to apply micro-cement to a bathroom floor. Covers surface preparation, primer, base coat, texture coat, and finishing sealer to produce a waterproof, seamless floor suitable for wet areas.
sourceNotes: Micro-cement application technique from UK trade practice. Manufacturer application guides for Topciment, Dune, and Ciment Studio ranges (generic referenced principles, not brand-specific instructions). UK building trade technical notes on cementitious coatings.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin-coat cementitious coating applied 2–3 mm thick over prepared substrates. Made from cement, polymer resins, and fine aggregates; the polymer binder makes it flexible enough to be applied over existing tiles and concrete without cracking under normal building movement. Applied in multiple thin coats (primer, base coat, textured coat, and sealer) each allowed to cure before the next. The finished surface is sealed to become waterproof and is suitable for wet-room floors and walls.

content.text: is a thin cementitious coating applied in layers over an existing tiled or concrete floor to give a seamless, jointless finish. The approach is priming, two or three product coats, and a penetrating sealer (each coat sanded between applications. The material is unforgiving of poor prep and requires practice to apply evenly; it is not a beginner project on a large bathroom floor.

Micro-cement needs a rigid, stable substrate with no flex. On timber joists, the deck must be double-sheeted with 18 mm plywood, screwed at 150 mm centres, so the combined thickness stops the deck flexing under foot traffic. On existing tiles, every loose tile must be fixed or removed; any hollow tiles will crack the coating above them. Degrease the existing surface and wash down; the primer bonds by chemical adhesion, not mechanical key.

Roll a manufacturer-specific primer onto the clean substrate using a short-nap roller. Allow to tack) typically 30 to 60 minutes (before coating. The primer must not dry fully before the base coat goes on. Follow the specific manufacturer's open time; working past it means re-priming.

Mix the base-coat material to a creamy consistency. Spread a thin layer) 1 to 1.5 mm (using the taping knife in broad sweeping strokes. Work in sections; the coating sets in 20 to 40 minutes and must not be overworked once it begins to stiffen. Press the knife edge firmly to skim across the surface rather than build up. Once dry, sand lightly with 80-grit, vacuum, and prime again before the second coat.
Apply the texture coat and sand

The second or third coat) the texture coat (creates the characteristic mineral surface. Apply as the base coat, but work the surface to develop texture as it stiffens: a light circular motion with a damp trowel or sponge creates a fine grain. Aim for even coverage with no pinholes visible. Sand at 120-grit when dry; this is the stage that determines smoothness. Vacuum thoroughly before sealing.

Apply two coats of a manufacturer-matched polyurethane or epoxy sealer) one for floors, not walls, as floor sealers carry anti-slip additives. Roll on with a short-nap roller, cutting in at edges with a brush. Allow 4 hours between coats. The sealed surface is waterproof and suitable for wet areas. Allow 3 days before wetting the floor; full cure is 7 days.

attrs.symptom: Hairline cracks appear in the coating within days of application
attrs.cause: Substrate movement, under-mixed material, or coats applied too thick.
attrs.fix: Identify and fix the substrate cause first. Re-apply at the correct 1–1.5 mm coat thickness; do not fill cracks in micro-cement with more micro-cement: the patch will show.

attrs.symptom: Pinholes visible in the finished coat
attrs.cause: Air bubbles in the coating rising to the surface during set.
attrs.fix: Sand back the pinhole layer, apply another thin coat at a slightly slower working pace to allow bubbles to escape, and re-seal.
